model_narrative: We place queens on a square chess board and decide, for each square, whether a queen sits on it. We want to put as many queens on the board as possible while keeping them in a non-attacking arrangement. The objective is to make the total number of placed queens as large as possible.

sets.name: i
sets.doc: the rows and columns of the square chess board, numbered from 1; the board is square so this same set indexes both the rank direction and the file direction, and a board square is a pair of one row and one column

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the diagonal bands of the board, one band per offset; for an n by n board there are 2n-1 diagonal bands running in each of the two diagonal directions

params.name: sh
params.doc: the column shift that selects which forward-sloping diagonal band a square lies on; for diagonal band s it equals the band number minus the board size plus one, so that band one is the longest corner-to-corner forward diagonal and the bands sweep across the board

params.name: rev
params.doc: a per-row reversal offset used to map a square onto its backward-sloping diagonal band; for row i it equals the board size plus one minus twice the row number

vars.name: x
vars.doc: 1 if a queen is placed on the square at that row and column, 0 otherwise

vars.name: tot
vars.doc: the total number of queens placed on the board

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Each row of the board must hold exactly one queen. For every row, the number of queens placed across that row's squares has to be exactly one.

constraints.name: x[i,
constraints.intent: Each column of the board must hold exactly one queen. For every column, the number of queens placed down that column's squares has to be exactly one.

constraints.name: i:
constraints.intent: No forward-sloping diagonal may hold more than one queen. For every forward diagonal band of the board, the queens placed on the squares that lie along that band can total at most one.

constraints.name: i:
constraints.intent: No backward-sloping diagonal may hold more than one queen. For every backward diagonal band of the board, the queens placed on the squares that lie along that band can total at most one.

constraints.name: tot
constraints.intent: Define the total count of placed queens as the number of squares that carry a queen, added up over the whole board, and set the total to equal that count.

query: We're looking at concentrating the formation toward the middle of the board — the proposal is to keep at least three queens inside the central four-by-four block spanning rows 3 through 6 and columns 3 through 6. What does the solver return for the layout, and does a full non-attacking board still come back?
expected_pyomo: model.central_cluster = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, j] for i in ['3', '4', '5', '6'] for j in ['3', '4', '5', '6']) >= 3)

query: There's a proposal on the table to keep the top row's queen positioned strictly to the right of the bottom row's queen — its column index at least one higher than the column of the queen in row 8. Plug that in and walk me through whether the board still completes and what arrangement it lands on.
expected_pyomo: model.row1_right_of_row8 = Constraint(expr=sum(int(j) * model.x['1', j] for j in model.i) >= sum(int(j) * model.x['8', j] for j in model.i) + 1)

query: We're evaluating a symmetry rule tying the two main corners together: a queen may sit on the top-left corner (row 1, column 1) only if one also sits on the bottom-right corner (row 8, column 8). Add that coupling and tell me what the optimizer returns for the placement.

query: Engineering is pitching a board where the entire main diagonal — every square whose row number equals its column number — is kept clear of queens. Run that across all eight diagonal squares and let me know whether a full layout still exists and how it arranges itself.

query: There's a proposal to lay the formation out off both grand diagonals at once — keep every square on the main diagonal clear and keep every square on the anti-diagonal (where the row and column numbers add to nine) clear as well. Apply both bans together and tell me what board the solver settles on.

query: The top-left quadrant of the board looks underused — rows 1 through 4, columns 1 through 4 only hold a couple of queens while the rest crowd the far side. Shouldn't at least three queens sit in that corner region? What's keeping the model from packing more of them up there?
expected_pyomo: model.crowd_top_left = Constraint(expr=sum(model.x[i, j] for i in ['1', '2', '3', '4'] for j in ['1', '2', '3', '4']) >= 3)

query: Reading down the board, the row-2 queen sits way out to the right while the row-3 queen sits well to its left — the columns jump backward between those two rows. Wouldn't it be tidier for the row-2 queen to land no further right than the row-3 queen? What's the model seeing that pulls them out of order?
expected_pyomo: model.row2_le_row3 = Constraint(expr=sum(int(j) * model.x['2', j] for j in model.i) <= sum(int(j) * model.x['3', j] for j in model.i))

query: The anti-diagonal running from the top-right toward the bottom-left — every square whose row and column numbers add up to nine — keeps catching queens in the plan. Couldn't we just hold that whole diagonal empty in each row? What's the tradeoff pulling queens back onto those squares?
